sentences.text: Well, we were in-- we arrived back in this town, and they-- the places, the rooms that we had been in were still vacant so we went-- we went back to those.
And-- but of course, my father was not with us.
He was still in the camp.
Now, he had told us that he would try to escape also, and he-- and he said-- that's what he told us.
Go back to this town, [INAUDIBLE], and, you know, when I escape, if I can, I'll join you-- I'll try to join you there.
Well-- and actually there were-- there was another family and another woman-- I told you there were three other people who escaped with us-- and they were also from this town.
I mean we went together, we escaped together.
One woman had a son who was about my age.
His name was Leon Silver.
And as a matter of fact, Leon and his parents had been on the as well.
That's where we first met them.
They had a cabin not far from where our cabin was.
Anyway, we were-- it was New Year's Day, and, I don't know, we were outside, and suddenly in the distance we saw these three men walking.
And my mother recognized my father.
My father had escaped on New Year's Eve.
We had escaped on Christmas Eve.
And so we-- again, we were reunited.
I mean talk about luck.
I-- I learned French rapidly.
By the time we were picked up to-- to be put in this camp, I already spoke French.
I learned it much faster in France than I did in Belgium I think because there were a lot of kids.
It was a small village.
I was playing with them and kids just pick up a language rapidly.
It was a-- I told you there were about 200 people living in this village.
It was a one-room schoolhouse.
All grades were in this one room, and there was one teacher who just taught all the grades.
And I remember it very well.
I remember exactly where I sat.
I remember there was a bookcase in the back which was called library.
And I remember the-- the name of the teacher, Madame [?
Picquel, ?] and she was terrific.
Her-- She was there with her husband.
They had also fled from Northern France I believe.
Her husband taught school in a village nearby.
They had escaped because the rumor was that they were communists, and of course, the Nazi's were looking for communists, also, to kill them.
Anyway, after we got back to the village, I have some very pleasant memories of that stay in France.
The people generally were very nice.
Although, I have to say that I don't know when, but soon after we arrived, some anti-Semitism began to be felt.
I mean I remember one kid in particular who would say 'dirty Jew' and stuff like that.
And that-- they didn't know anything about this before because there were no Jews in this town.
They didn't know what a Jew-- The only thing they knew about Jews was what they had read in the Bible.
And when we told them we were Jewish, they stared at us, 'what do you mean you're Jewish?' I mean they had this conception that Jews had horns or something.
Really.
I mean I'm not kidding.
And they couldn't-- they couldn't fathom the fact that we were Jews and that we looked just like anybody else.
But they-- they really, they treated us well.
Food was a big problem because the Germans were requisitioning all the food that they could get for Germany and for the German Army.
And of course, we were in a village which was very helpful, because food was being produced right there.
But you know, they had a certain quota.
They had to send food to Paris and to the German Army.
There wasn't much left.
And of course, a lot of it they kept to feed themselves.
And we had to buy it.
Now, how do we get any money?
Well, when they found out that my father was a tailor, they asked him to-- to-- to work, because there was no tailor for miles around, or I should say kilometers.
And he said, well, I can't work.
I don't have a sewing machine.
So one of the people in the village had a sewing machine.
She gave it to my father.
He set up shop and he-- he was working, and it really was-- was terrific because we had some money. you weren't able to get much for that money, but we had some.
And yeah, so food was a problem.
We really-- there was very little food to eat.
You know, my mother would go and buy some bread, and rarely, I guess, we would have maybe an egg or something.
I mean-- and vegetables.
But there wasn't a lot to eat.
As a matter of fact, I-- [LAUGH] I remember, it was, I guess, the-- the Rosh Hashanah after we actually-- no.
There must have been two Rosh Hashanahs.
It must have been 1941.
My mother was saying we're going to have a holiday, and we don't have-- we really don't have any food.

So Leon, this friend of mine who had been on the also I told you about before, and I decided that we would go to one of the villages up on the mountain where almost nobody went, and we would see if we could find [LAUGH] some food.
I mean here we were, we were 11-- he was 12 then.
I was-- yeah, 11.
No, no, no.
I'm sorry.
I guess I was 8 or 9, and he was 10 or 11.
And here we were going to get food.
Well, we walk up to this village-- it was probably two or three miles-- and we were actually able to buy some flour, I think half a dozen eggs, and some honey, and something else.
Our parents had given us-- imagine-- trusted us-- and trusted us with money.
Anyway, we-- we-- we got all this stuff and we brought it home.
Well, you can't imagine what our parents were like when they saw that we had brought the stuff.
I mean my mother was ecstatic.
She said, 'Oh, my god!
I'm gonna be able to bake a cake for the holiday!' [LAUGH] It was-- it was just-- it was amazing.
Imagine, you know, here we are, we can go to the store and buy whatever we want.
So it's hard to imagine just not being able to find food.
I also learned how to ride a bicycle there because the teacher had a bike and she allowed some of the kids to use it.
And so I learned how to ride a bike in-- in [INAUDIBLE] also.
So there was some nice things that were-- that I have memories of.
But you know, we were constantly afraid because we knew-- we heard the news, the Germans were attacking Russia, and God only knew when they were going to take over all of France.
You know we just wanted to get out of Europe, away from-- from the Germans.
Well, Southern France I told you was, quote 'free' or Vichy France, and as such had diplomatic relations with the United States.
The consulate was in Marseille.
So my father was able to go to this consulate and-- and ask about our visa number to the United States and pursue that, which was extremely fortunate.
Because in the Spring of 1942 we-- we-- our number came up.
We got a visa to the United States, which was wonderful, except for one thing.
It was in the middle of the war.
How were we going to get to the United States?
I mean there were no planes.
The only way to go was a boat, but it was the middle of the war.
Where were you gonna get a boat?

Well, the only place-- the only two countries in-- well, there were three countries, actually, in Europe that were neutral, and those countries were Sweden, Spain-- Spain had just gone through the Civil War, so they weren't interested in joining any other war-- Spain, and Portugal.
So the only ship that you could go on would be a neutral ship because if you went on a French ship it was very dangerous that you might be fired on by U-boats.
So anyway, we actually managed to get passage on a ship from Portugal leaving Lisbon for the United States in-- at the end of-- that was at the end of May or the middle of May of 1942.
I guess it was the end of May.
But then the problem was how to get to Portugal, because there was Spain in between where we were and Portugal.
Well-- so we had to try to get a transit visa.
Well, we did.
We finally got a transit visa and we got on the train and we traveled through Spain.
And I-- I remember that trip because I saw all the desolation, ruined buildings.
I mean this was just a couple years after the end of the Civil War in Spain.
It was terrible.
We stayed in Madrid one night overnight and then we took the train to Lisbon from Madrid.
No.
No.
But I-- I-- I-- I'm pretty sure that we-- for that stuff we got money from the United States.
As soon as we heard that we had a visa, we wired our relatives to send us money.
Because we knew that even if they didn't have any money, they would go out and borrow it, because this was a matter of life or death.
We got to Lisbon, and I remember-- I remember something about Lisbon.
I remember the streets.
It was-- the streets were sort of mosaics, the sidewalks.
They had little stones.
It's funny that you remember things like that.
Anyway, we-- we-- we got on the ship.
It was towards-- some time towards the end of May-- I don't remember the exact date.
And the name of the ship was the And this was the exact opposite of the which was a luxury liner.
This ship was a small boat.
I don't know how many passengers it would normally hold.
I think it was a transport-- a ship-- it may have been a large fishing ship or it may-- no, that doesn't sound right.
They had large areas, large holds, so I guess it was for transporting goods from one place to another.
And I don't know how many passengers there were, but there were quite a few.
My mother and the women were in the one huge room on deck, but my father and I and all the men were in the hold, right near the engine.
Well, we-- you know, we were happy to be on the ship, but I was seasick on that ship from the moment we left Lisbon to the moment we got to New York.
And of course it was terrible.
You couldn't-- you couldn't sleep in the hold because it was hot as hell, and people were vomiting all the time, and the smell was just terrible.
So when it wasn't pouring we would sleep on the deck just because-- because it was impossible to sleep in-- in the-- in the hold.
We stop in Bermuda for about a week and all the passengers were interrogated by British Intelligence, which was interesting.
Even I-- the kids went and were interrogated.
And I remember that.
Well, you know, I felt really important.
Here was this solider, he was interrogating me.
He wanted to know if I knew anything that would be of interest as far as military operations were concerned.
So he asked me specific questions.
And I remembered he asked me if there was a power plant near where we lived in France, and I-- I-- I said yes, there is, and I told him about it and where exactly it was and so on.
I felt very important.
So we were in Bermuda for a week, and then we set sail and we arrived in New York on June 24, 1942.
We lived-- we stayed in New York for a month.
Yes.
Oh, yes.
I'm glad you reminded me.

I-- you know, we had to-- we had to pass through customs, but the kids were running around, and we were running around in the-- there was sort of a hangar-like area in the pier where all our luggage was.
And where actually, we ran outside, too.
There was a large crowd of people apparently awaiting the passengers on the ship.
And I had seen pictures of my great uncle and my grandfather who were in New York.
And I thought that they might be there, and I looked and I thought I recognized them, but I didn't have the guts to go up and say I'm Sol Messinger.
Are you so and so?
It turned out that they were-- that was my grandfather and my great uncle.

The other thing that I remember was we had gone through customs and-- and a friend of mine and I were-- this friend had lived in [INAUDIBLE], in the-- the town near our village, and they were on the ship too.
And-- we-- you know, we were good friends.
And after we had cleared customs, we had-- we each had French comic books.
And so we decided we would exchange comic books.
And the-- one of the guards saw this.
And he-- he took us and he-- he asked us what we were doing, or somebody must have spoken in French or German because I understood.
And-- or he asked our parents what-- what-- what-- he thought we were maybe exchanging information.
And my-- I remember my mother, she could have killed me.
She almost-- I mean here we were, and she was afraid that they were gonna send us back because we had-- we had exchanged comic books.
Well, we explained what we were doing and we got off.
We-- I mean we got out and we stayed with my great uncle and my great aunt for a month.
And now my father's brother, who-- whom my father had worked for in Germany was already in New York.
They were living in Washington Heights at that time and-- and we were in Brooklyn.
And-- And my father couldn't find a job in New York.

So we came to Buffalo because my-- my father's two sisters, both of whom had been in Cuba at the time the arrived there, and my cousin Edith who was the daughter of one of them, Frieda-- had moved-- had left Cuba.
They had gotten a visa to the United States the year before and they had come to Buffalo because of some other relatives who lived there.
You know, that's-- that's why you went to certain places.
You knew somebody.
You wanted to go someplace where you knew somebody and where you knew somebody might be able to help you.
So we came to Buffalo.
It was the end of July of 1942.
And my-- my parents in their wisdom decided that what I needed was fresh air.

So three days after we arrived in Buffalo they sent me to the Jewish Fresh Air Camp, which was a terrible experience, because number one, I didn't speak English, so I couldn't communicate with anybody.
And two, I didn't know anybody.
And it was-- So about an hour after I arrived at this camp, I started to cry, and I cried for a week until they came to pick me up.
However, you know, the story has a nice side to it because at that camp, I met two guys who were my age who spoke some Yiddish, so we were able to communicate a little.
And those two guys are still two of my best friends today.
One lives in Boston, one lives in Buffalo, one is a lawyer, one's a doctor.
So-- And then in September I started school.
And by November, I was speaking English fluently.
What they did was they asked me what grade I was in and I told them I was in fifth grade.
So they put me in fifth grade.
Well, you know, I didn't understand a word the teacher was saying.
I couldn't follow anything.
So they-- the principal decided that she would put me in first grade, and then as I learned a little they would put me in second grade until I worked my way up to the fifth grade again.
This, by the way, was School 53.
It was in the old-- what was called the Humboldt section on the East side.
It was a lovely section at that time-- very-- a very Jewish section.
And-- and so by November I was-- I was back in fifth grade, I was speaking English fluently.
I couldn't understand why my parents weren't speaking English fluently.
I mean I was, why weren't they?
Well, of course, I didn't realize that it was much more difficult for adults to learn the language, but they-- they eventually learned it, my mother much better than my father.
And then, of course, they spoke with an accent.
And-- and I began-- I began to live a normal life, which I had not lived until then.
Did your parents get jobs?
My father got a job as a tailor.
He worked very, very hard.
I mean he would work 18 hours a day sometimes.
My mother didn't work until I was about to start college, and we need-- we needed money.
I got a scholarship for college, but we needed-- I needed extra money anyway.
So she started working.
She-- she worked as a sales lady.
She sold women's clothes, and she was a terrific saleswoman.
And she worked until she was 75.
I do want to say a couple of other things.
All my relatives in Poland-- my grandparents, three aunts, a lot of cousins, and a huge extended family, second cousins and so on, they all died.
My grandparents and two aunts who lived in this little village of [INAUDIBLE] we understand died.
Well, one aunt, and my grandparents died in Treblinka in the concentration camp.
One aunt, Flora, apparently escaped from Treblinka-- we found this out after the war, and went back to the little town where-- where they had lived all their lives.
And one of the inhabitants there shot her.
And she didn't die in the concentration camp.
So they all died.
My mother's brother Erin also died.
I mean we tried to find him after the war, and we-- we couldn't.
And remember I told you that a friend of mine, Leon Silver, he lived in Brussels with us, and then we fled together to France and we lived in [INAUDIBLE] together, and we were on the together.
Of course, they-- they-- they were in France.
And in October of 1942, three or four months after we left, the Germans came and they picked up-- they picked up all the Jews and sent them to Auschwitz.

And we heard after the war, because we communicated with this teacher-- my teacher, his teacher-- that his parents found out that-- somehow that they were going to be taken, and they asked the teacher to hide their son, Leon.
And they did.
They hid him.
But he was very attached to his mother.
And two days after the-- the Germans had picked up his parents, he just left the school and he went to the [FRENCH], to the police station.
And he said, he told them he wanted to join his parents, and he did in Auschwitz.
Another, as far as survivors are concerned, my mother had another brother who was living in Holland-- this was the youngest brother, [FRENCH].
And we had-- she hadn't seen him for years, but we had-- she had corresponded with him.
After the war we tried to find him through the Red Cross, but we couldn't, they couldn't locate him.
And so we assumed, of course, that he had been killed as well.
And I traveled in Europe in 1963.
I was in Holland, and-- you know, I knew that my uncle had lived there, but I-- I-- I assumed he was dead.
A few years later, my great uncle, my mother's uncle was also traveling in Europe.
He was in Holland, and he was passing through Utrecht, which is where my uncle had lived.
I don't know what possessed him.
He opened the telephone book and looked for the name.
His name, because he was adopted, was Humburger.
And-- and he found a name and he called him, and it was him.
He had married and he had children by that time.
I mean-- And then of course when he got back, he called us from New York and told us.
I mean can you imagine how we felt?
I mean this was 20 years after the war when we found out that my uncle was still alive.
It was incredible!
And so I found that I had three cousins that I never knew, and of course now we know each other very well.
So that-- that is one of the happy parts of this whole story.
Well, and the other happy part, of course, is that we survived in, as I said, something that I can only call a miracle.
I-- I went to public school, I went to high school here in Buffalo.
Then I went to college at the University of Buffalo.
And I went to medical school at the University of Buffalo.
And I took my internship in training here in Buffalo.
Then I served in the Army for a couple years afterwards as a pathologist, as a doctor.
And then I came back to Buffalo and I've worked here ever since.
Yes.
No.
No.
I have never gone back to Germany and I have no intention of going back to Germany.
I don't-- I still speak the language, and it comes in handy sometimes, but I really don't like to speak it and I really don't like to hear it.

And when I see somebody speaking German, visiting, or if I'm in Europe and I hear German spoken, I look at these people, and if they'd old, I always think to myself I wonder what they were doing in the war, especially the men.

So I've never gone back there, but I went back to this little village that I-- we lived in in France, [INAUDIBLE], And it was strange coming back because I remember-- as I told you, I remembered everything.
I remembered exactly where the streets were and so on.
Except everything seemed so small, including the schoolhouse.
And I went in and there was recess and the kids were playing and I spoke to the teacher and I told her who I was and I'd like to go into the school.
And I went in and nothing had changed.
Nothing.
My desk was where it was.
I think it was the same desk.
This was, I don't know, 30 years later [LAUGH].
The library, the bookcase was still there.
It was really wonderful to go back to that town.
It revived a lot of memories, but I've-- I had good memories of that.
And the other place I went was the little village or shtetl where my grandparents came from.
And that was not such a pleasant experience.
I was in Krakow and I-- I hired a car with-- from-- with a guy who spoke some English, and we went to this town.
First we came to [INAUDIBLE], and my grandmother, my mother's mother, was-- is buried there.
She died when my mother was very young.
And so I asked them to find the Jewish cemetery.
And they-- the people said, well, we don't know where it is.
Anyway, to make a long story short, it turned out that the Germans had destroyed the cemetery, and after the war, the Poles had built a post office on the side of the cemetery.
I went back to the little town where my father had lived, my grandparents there, and I found the house where he had lived.
And there was this old lady sitting on the porch next door and I went over and we-- she remembered my grandparents.

And I-- I spoke through this interpreter which was extremely frustrating, and we did this for about five minutes, and then in my frustration I said-- I yelled, 'Doesn't anybody speak English here?' And of course, they looked at me as if I were crazy.
And then I said in German, 'Doesn't anybody speak German?' And this old lady said, 'Of course I speak German.' I'd forgotten that that was part of Austria before World War I.
So we were able to have this long conversation, and she remembered my aunts, my grandparents.
She told me what happened, you know, when they were picked up by the Germans.

I couldn't tear myself away from this woman because while I was talking to her, and she was-- she must have been 85 at that time-- I had the feeling that my parents were still-- and my grandparents were still alive.
